title: Cursor AI
content: Cursor AI Cursor AI is most effective when you treat it like a fast engineering assistant: give it narrow context, a concrete objective, and a clear definition of done. 1. How to Use Cursor AI 1.1 Start with a precise task State the file, feature, bug, or refactor target explicitly. Tell it what success looks like: code change, explanation, test, or review. Good prompt example: "Update `authService.js` to refresh tokens on 401 and add unit tests." 1.2 Use it for the right job Use chat for explanation, planning, debugging hypotheses, and review. Use inline editing when you already know the exact block that must change. Use codebase-aware prompts when the task depends on multiple files or architecture. 1.3 Follow an efficient workflow Ask Cursor to inspect the relevant files first. Have it explain the current behavior in short form. Then ask for a focused patch instead of a broad rewrite. Review the diff and run tests before accepting the result. 1.4 Good prompt patterns Bug fix: "Find why form submission fails after page refresh and patch only the root cause." Refactor: "Extract repeated API error handling into one helper without changing behavior." Review: "Review this file for bugs, edge cases, and missing tests." Learning: "Explain this function line by line and identify the risky parts." 2. How to Use It Effectively 2.1 Keep scope narrow Do not ask for "fix the whole app" when the issue is in one module. Point to the file, error message, or failing behavior. Narrow prompts reduce irrelevant output and improve patch quality. 2.2 Give constraints early Specify framework, coding style, libraries, and files that must not change. Say whether you want minimal edits, no new dependencies, or test coverage. Clear constraints prevent large, expensive, low-signal responses. 2.3 Iterate instead of dumping everything at once First ask for analysis. Then ask for a small code change. Then ask for tests or cleanup if needed. This stepwise flow improves control and reduces wasted tokens. 2.4 Always verify the result Read the diff instead of accepting changes blindly. Ask Cursor to explain why each change is necessary. Run tests, linters, or the app itself before merging. 2.5 Supply context selectively Share only the functions, logs, or files that matter. Summarize long history instead of pasting entire conversations. Relevant context improves answer quality more than large context. 3. How to Save Tokens 3.1 Ask for short outputs by default Say: "Answer in 5 bullets" or "Give only the patch." Ask for detailed explanations only when you need them. Short outputs directly reduce token usage. 3.2 Reuse context instead of repeating it Do not restate the same project background in every prompt. Reference the earlier instruction and add only the delta. Example: "Use the same file and continue from the previous fix, now add tests." 3.3 Paste less code Share only the relevant function, component, stack trace, or error log. If a file is large, identify the section and summarize the rest. Large pasted files often increase cost without increasing accuracy. 3.4 Split complex tasks into smaller prompts One huge prompt often produces a huge answer. Break the work into inspect, patch, test, and review stages. Smaller rounds usually cost fewer tokens and are easier to control. 3.5 Ask for diffs, summaries, and decisions Prefer "show the exact changed lines" over "rewrite the whole file." Prefer "summarize the bug in 3 lines" over long narrative analysis. Ask for options only when a decision is actually needed. 3.6 Maintain a compact working style A practical low-token formula: Task: one sentence Context: one short paragraph or one file reference Constraints: 2 to 4 bullets Output format: patch, bullets, or short explanation 4. Common Mistakes to Avoid 4.1 Being too vague Prompts like "improve this" or "fix my project" force the model to guess your intent, which usually wastes tokens and produces weak results. 4.2 Requesting full rewrites too early Full rewrites are expensive, risky, and harder to review. Ask for a minimal patch first unless the code is truly beyond repair. 4.3 Asking for explanation and implementation in one giant prompt Split it into two steps: first understand the issue, then request the code change. This keeps both the input and output compact. 4.4 Trusting generated code without review Cursor speeds up implementation, but correctness still depends on reviewing the diff, validating behavior, and running tests.

title: Database
content: Database RSL - row level security Row level security controls which rows a user can read, insert, update, or delete from a database table. Instead of protecting only the whole table, access rules are applied row by row. Why It Is Used To ensure users can access only their own records. To isolate tenant data in multi-tenant applications. To enforce security rules directly inside the database. To reduce the risk of accidental data exposure from application code. Common Example In a notes application, every note may have a user_id. A row level security policy can allow a logged-in user to view only rows where user_id matches their own user id. Important Points Enable row level security on tables that store user-specific or sensitive data. Create separate policies for read, insert, update, and delete actions. Test policies with different user roles before moving to production. Do not rely only on frontend checks for data protection.

title: DataBricks
content: Databricks Notes certification What is the difference between a Databricks Certification and a Databricks Accreditation?". Databricks offers two types of tests you can take to validate your skills and earn digital badges shareable with social networks: Certifications and Accreditations. Below is the difference between the two: Certification: A certification is a proctored exam completed through our online examination portal, Webassessor. These exams have stricter rules and requirements to complete, have an associated cost, and a retake policy (you must wait 14 days between unsuccessful exam attempts). Accreditation: Accreditations are free, non-proctored quizzes completed through the Databricks Academy. These have fewer rules and can be attempted as many times as needed (they do not have a re-take policy). Databricks Exam Vendor Databricks utilizes a third-party vendor to proctor certification exams - exams are proctored to ensure that testing is fair and consistent across all examinees. For this, Databricks uses Kryterion Webassessor, also referred to as Webassessor, a common vendor for exam proctoring in the technology training industry. Below we include key information about Webassessor you need to know as a Databricks certification exam candidate. Webassessor Test Taker Account If you haven't already done so, you'll need to create a Webassessor Test Taker account for Databricks. Even if you've created a Webassessor account for another company that uses Webassessor as a proctoring service, you'll still need to create one for Databricks. Keep in mind that whatever email address you use for your Webassessor account will be linked to your certifications and badges, so be cautious of using a company email for this account in case you have plans of changing employers in the future. Databricks components Workspace A Databricks workspace is the collaborative environment where users create, organize, and run notebooks, jobs, queries, dashboards, and machine learning experiments. Mosaic AI Agent Framework remember that it is a framework , and not a databricks component. Mosaic AI Agent Framework is Databricks' framework for building, testing, deploying, and monitoring production AI agents. It lets teams create agent applications that use LLMs, tools, retrieval, and business logic while staying integrated with Databricks governance and serving infrastructure. Author agents using common frameworks such as LangChain, LangGraph, DSPy, OpenAI SDK, or custom Python code. Connect agents to enterprise data and tools through Unity Catalog functions, Mosaic AI Vector Search, model serving endpoints, and other governed Databricks assets. Use MLflow tracing and logging to capture prompts, tool calls, intermediate steps, responses, and evaluation results for debugging and auditability. Evaluate agent quality with Agent Evaluation before moving applications into production. Deploy agents to Mosaic AI Model Serving so they can be called as scalable REST endpoints. In short, the framework helps move an agent from notebook prototype to governed production application with traceability, evaluation, deployment, and monitoring built into the Databricks platform. MLflow MLflow is an open source platform integrated with Databricks for managing the machine learning lifecycle. It helps teams track experiments, package code, register models, evaluate outputs, and move models or GenAI applications into production with governance and reproducibility. ® Manage end-to-end ML and GenAl workflows, from development to production. ® Unified platform for both traditional ML and GenAl applications. Track parameters, metrics, artifacts, datasets, prompts, and traces so model behavior can be compared and audited across runs. Use the MLflow Model Registry with Databricks to manage model versions, stage transitions, approvals, and deployment readiness. mlflow.pyfunc mlflow.pyfunc is MLflow's generic Python model flavor. It lets you wrap custom inference logic in a standard predict() interface, so the model can be logged, loaded, evaluated, registered, and deployed consistently even when it is not tied to a specific framework like scikit-learn or TensorFlow.In Databricks (via MLflow), pyfunc.predict is the method you call on a loaded PyFunc model to perform inference. mlflow.genai.evaluate() mlflow.genai.evaluate() is MLflow's API for systematically evaluating GenAI applications such as RAG assistants and AI agents. It runs your model against test data, applies evaluation scorers for criteria such as factual accuracy, completeness, and usefulness, and logs results so quality can be tracked over time. The function requires three main components: a dataset of test cases, scorers that define the evaluation criteria, and the model's predict() function that generates responses. Its key importance is support for human Subject Matter Expert (SME) feedback alongside automated evaluation. Pure LLM-as-judge approaches can miss domain-specific nuances, so MLflow lets teams integrate calibrated expert judgments directly into the evaluation workflow. SMEs can review and score responses using custom rubrics, and these human annotations are stored alongside automated metrics. This hybrid approach combines the scale of automation with the domain accuracy of human experts, helping evaluations capture real customer-support quality signals that automated metrics alone may miss. Teams can also validate that automated LLM judges align with expert standards by training or calibrating them against SME annotations. Feature Store A Feature Store is a centralized repository that stores and manages reusable features (data attributes) used by AI, machine learning, and Generative AI applications. In Databricks, a Feature Store is typically built on top of Delta tables and provides additional capabilities such as feature discovery, governance, lineage tracking, and consistent access to features during both training and inference. For example, in a customer support chatbot, features like a user's subscription tier, preferred language, and account status can be stored in the Feature Store and retrieved to personalize prompts sent to an LLM. Similarly, in a recommendation system, features such as purchase history, average spending, and product preferences can be shared across multiple models instead of being recreated each time. In GenAI applications, the Feature Store often acts as a reliable source of structured context that complements unstructured data retrieved through RAG systems. A Feature Store is a centralized place to create, discover, reuse, and govern machine learning features. Features are curated data columns, such as customer lifetime value, average transaction amount, or recent login count, that are used as inputs for model training and prediction. In Databricks, feature tables are usually stored as Delta tables and governed through Unity Catalog. This helps data scientists and engineers share trusted features across teams while keeping permissions, lineage, and documentation consistent. Training consistency: The same feature definitions can be used for model training and batch or real-time inference, reducing training-serving skew. Reuse: Teams can search for existing features instead of rebuilding the same transformations in multiple projects. Governance: Feature ownership, access control, lineage, and metadata can be managed with the rest of the lakehouse data. Serving support: Features can be published for low-latency online lookup when models need fresh values during prediction. Databricks also supports storing vector embeddings as features. Embeddings (for example, 768‑dimensional vectors from a text encoder) can be stored in Delta tables using array or map column types, which the Feature Store treats like any other feature column. [web:11][web:12][web:15] These embedding features can then be reused across GenAI workloads (such as RAG pipelines or similarity-based recommendations) and can also be indexed with Databricks Vector Search for fast semantic lookup during inference. [web:13][web:16][web:20] eature stores handle tasks like feature engineering, versioning, and sharing, making them great for managing structured data. Vector databases, on the other hand, specialize in fast similarity searches for high-dimensional embeddings, offering better performance for those use cases. Each serves a distinct role in the machine learning pipeline. Compute Compute provides the processing resources used to run notebooks, workflows, SQL warehouses, and other workloads. It includes all-purpose clusters, job clusters, and SQL warehouses. Delta Lake Delta Lake is the storage layer used for reliable lakehouse tables. It supports ACID transactions, schema enforcement, scalable metadata handling, and time travel. Unity Catalog Unity Catalog centralizes governance for data and AI assets. It manages catalogs, schemas, tables, views, volumes, models, permissions, lineage, and auditing. It allows you to apply fine-grained access control and usage policies based on user roles, groups, or specific conditions. It provides a single governance layer across Databricks workspaces, so teams can discover, secure, monitor, and share data consistently. Instead of managing permissions separately in every workspace, administrators define access rules once and apply them across the lakehouse. Unity Catalog provides a single governance solution for both data and AI assets, enabling end-to-end lineage between them. It uses a unified permission model and an open data sharing protocol, making it easy to share models with third parties or outside organizations. Managing models in Unity Catalog means assets are organized, secure, and can be shared flexibly while maintaining traceability and control throughout their lifecycle. Metastore: The top-level container for Unity Catalog metadata. A metastore stores information about catalogs, schemas, tables, views, storage locations, permissions, and lineage. Catalog: The first level in the three-level namespace. It groups related schemas and is commonly used to separate business domains, environments, or teams. Schema: The second level in the namespace. It organizes tables, views, functions, models, and volumes inside a catalog. Tables and views: Unity Catalog controls access to managed tables, external tables, and views. Views can be used to expose filtered or masked data to different users. Volumes: Volumes govern non-tabular files such as CSV, JSON, images, libraries, and other files stored in cloud object storage. Fine-grained security: You can grant permissions at the catalog, schema, table, view, row, column, or function level. This supports use cases such as column masking, row-level filtering, and role-based access control. Data lineage: Unity Catalog captures lineage for tables, columns, notebooks, jobs, dashboards, and workflows. This helps teams understand where data came from, how it changed, and which downstream assets depend on it. Auditing and compliance: Access events and governance actions can be tracked for security reviews, compliance reporting, and troubleshooting. Data sharing: Unity Catalog works with Delta Sharing to securely share data with users inside or outside an organization without copying the data. Workflows Workflows schedule and orchestrate production jobs. They can run notebooks, Python scripts, JARs, dbt tasks, Delta Live Tables pipelines, and other task types. Databricks AI functions Databricks AI functions let you call AI capabilities directly from SQL or Spark workflows for tasks like document parsing, extraction, classification, and preparing content for search. ai_parse_document() parses binary document content such as PDFs, images, Word files, and PowerPoint files into a structured VARIANT result. The output can include pages, elements, text, tables, figures, layout metadata, bounding boxes, and error or run metadata. Gen AI course https://partner-academy.databricks.com/learn/learning-plans/315/generative-ai-engineering-pathway?hash=2f3bcdb1da62897d25e7f26e1079acaba9b1ce78&generated_by=1525279 self study and other resources Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis, or ABSA, is a more detailed form of sentiment analysis. Instead of only deciding whether the whole text is positive, negative, or neutral, ABSA identifies specific aspects mentioned in the text and finds the sentiment for each aspect. For example, in the review "The model response was accurate, but the answer was too slow", normal sentiment analysis may mark the review as mixed. ABSA breaks it into aspects: accuracy is positive, while latency is negative. In Gen AI applications, ABSA is useful for analyzing user feedback at a finer level. Teams can understand whether users are unhappy with answer correctness, response speed, tone, formatting, hallucinations, retrieval quality, or safety behavior. This makes it easier to improve the right part of the AI system instead of treating all negative feedback the same. Aspect extraction: find the specific topic or feature mentioned in the feedback. Sentiment classification: classify sentiment for each aspect as positive, negative, or neutral. Root cause analysis: connect negative aspects to system components such as retrieval, generation, latency, grounding, or guardrails. overview of different LLM models LLMs differ in size, architecture, modality, specialization, context window, latency, cost, and deployment model. Choosing the right model is usually a trade-off between answer quality, speed, governance, and operating cost. General-purpose chat and reasoning models These models are designed for broad language tasks such as question answering, summarization, writing, planning, coding help, and multi-step reasoning. They are a good default when the task is open-ended or when users may ask many kinds of questions. Instruction-tuned models Instruction-tuned models are trained to follow natural language directions. They are useful for assistants, enterprise workflows, extraction tasks, classification, and prompt-based automation where the model must reliably follow a requested format or policy. Code models Code-focused models are optimized for generating, explaining, translating, debugging, and reviewing code. They are commonly used in developer assistants, notebook copilots, SQL generation, test generation, and migration work. Embedding models Embedding models convert text, images, or other content into vectors that capture semantic meaning. They are usually used for search, recommendation, clustering, duplicate detection, and retrieval augmented generation rather than direct chat responses. Multimodal models Multimodal models can work with more than one input type, such as text, images, audio, video, or documents. They are useful for document understanding, chart interpretation, image analysis, call analysis, and workflows where important context is not only plain text. Small language models Small language models trade some capability for lower cost, lower latency, and easier deployment. They are useful for high-volume classification, routing, extraction, summarization, edge deployment, or tasks with narrow and well-defined behavior. Open-weight and proprietary models Open-weight models can often be hosted, fine-tuned, or governed inside an organization's own environment. Proprietary hosted models often provide strong out-of-the-box quality, managed scaling, and rapid feature updates. The best choice depends on data sensitivity, compliance, customization needs, cost, and operational complexity. Use a stronger reasoning model for complex, ambiguous, or high-value tasks. Use a smaller or specialized model for repetitive, narrow, high-volume tasks. Use embedding models with a vector index when the system must search private or domain-specific knowledge. Use multimodal models when the answer depends on images, PDFs, screenshots, charts, audio, or video. Compare models using task-specific evaluation data, not only public benchmark scores. info of some LLM A. Dolly 1.5B Dolly 1.5B is a relatively small instruction-following language model released by Databricks for research and educational purposes. It was designed to demonstrate how an open model can be fine-tuned to follow natural language instructions. Due to its smaller parameter count, it requires fewer computational resources than larger models but offers more limited reasoning and generation capabilities. B. OpenAI GPT-5 GPT-5 is a large-scale language model developed by OpenAI. It is designed for advanced reasoning, content generation, coding assistance, question answering, summarization, and conversational AI applications. GPT-5 supports complex workflows, long-context understanding, and multimodal capabilities, making it suitable for enterprise and consumer-facing AI systems. C. BGE-large BGE-large (BAAI General Embedding) is an embedding model developed by the Beijing Academy of Artificial Intelligence (BAAI) for semantic search and retrieval tasks. Instead of generating text responses, it converts text into numerical vector representations that capture semantic meaning. These embeddings are commonly used in vector databases for similarity search, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), recommendation systems, clustering, and information retrieval applications. This is not commonly used for text generation. D. Llama2-70B Llama 2 70B is a large open-weight language model developed by Meta. It contains approximately 70 billion parameters and is designed for high-quality text generation, reasoning, summarization, coding, and conversational tasks. Organizations often choose Llama 2 70B when they require greater control over deployment, customization, and governance compared to fully hosted proprietary models.Llama 2 70B is not strictly open source according to the strict Open Source Initiative (OSI) definition. the license has restrictions (like requiring approval for users with >700M monthly active users), which disqualifies it from strict OSI open source certification. study of packages to prepare for GEN AI certification Tika Tika is a powerful library for content extraction that supports both digital PDFs and scanned image-based PDFs by integrating Optical Character Recognition (OCR). This makes it the most appropriate choice in this scenario where the documents vary in structure, and some are scanned images. Tika is versatile and can handle complex PDF layouts while extracting only the textual content. It also works well with large-scale document processing, which is crucial given the number of PDFs you are dealing with. PyMuPDF (also known as fitz) PyMuPDF is designed for handling PDFs, not DOCX files. While it can be effective for PDF content extraction, it is not applicable to Word document processing. python-docx python-docx is a Python library used for reading, creating, and updating Microsoft Word DOCX files. It is a suitable choice when the source documents are Word files because it can extract paragraphs, tables, headings, and other structured document content. However, it does not handle PDFs or scanned images, so it is best used alongside PDF-focused tools such as Tika or PyMuPDF when processing mixed document types. pytesseract pytesseract is a Python wrapper for the Tesseract OCR engine and is used to extract text from images. It is particularly useful when documents are scanned PDFs, screenshots, or image files that contain text but do not have an underlying text layer. By converting images into machine-readable text, pytesseract enables downstream NLP and GenAI workflows such as document indexing, summarization, and question answering. However, it focuses only on OCR and does not provide advanced document structure extraction like tables, headings, or metadata. For best results, it is often used together with libraries such as Tika, PyMuPDF, or python-docx when processing a mix of scanned and digital documents. Prompt Coverage Prompt Coverage measures whether a prompt fully covers the important instructions, constraints, data requirements, and output expectations needed for the task. It helps check if the prompt gives the model enough guidance to answer correctly and consistently. For example, if a support chatbot prompt says "answer customer questions" but does not mention tone, grounding, escalation rules, safety restrictions, or the expected response format, then prompt coverage is low. A high-coverage prompt clearly defines the role, allowed data sources, refusal behavior, formatting, and edge cases. In Gen AI evaluation, prompt coverage is useful for finding gaps before production. Poor coverage can cause hallucinations, inconsistent answers, unsafe responses, or outputs that downstream systems cannot parse. Instruction coverage: checks whether the task, role, and goal are clearly defined. Constraint coverage: checks whether rules such as grounding, safety, privacy, and refusal behavior are included. Output coverage: checks whether format, structure, length, and required fields are specified. Beam Search Decoder Beam Search Decoder is a decoding strategy used by language models to choose output text. Instead of always selecting only the single most likely next token, beam search keeps multiple possible sequences at each step and continues expanding the best candidates. The number of candidate sequences kept is called the beam width. For example, if the beam width is 3, the decoder tracks the top three possible output paths, scores them, and finally returns the sequence with the best overall score. Beam search can produce more coherent and optimized answers than greedy decoding because it considers future token combinations, not just the best immediate token. However, it can also reduce creativity and may produce repetitive or overly safe text if the search is too narrow or the scoring favors common phrases. Beam search can be used in AI applications like QA bots, summarizers, and support assistants when the goal is to produce stable and high-confidence answers. It is useful when consistency matters more than creativity. However, for real-time QA bots, beam search may increase latency because the model has to evaluate multiple candidate sequences. Many modern chatbot applications instead use sampling settings like temperature and top-p, or lower-temperature decoding, depending on the required balance between speed, accuracy, and response variety. Greedy decoding: selects the best next token at each step. Beam search: keeps several likely sequences and chooses the best full sequence. Higher beam width: explores more options but increases compute cost and latency. Top P and Top K Top P and Top K are decoding parameters used to control how a language model selects the next token. They are commonly used in AI applications like QA bots, chatbots, summarizers, and content generation tools to balance accuracy, creativity, and consistency. Top K limits the model to choosing from only the K most likely next tokens. For example, if top_k is 50, the model only considers the top 50 candidate tokens and ignores the rest. A lower Top K makes responses more focused and predictable, while a higher Top K allows more variety. Top P, also called nucleus sampling, selects from the smallest group of tokens whose combined probability reaches the value of P. For example, if top_p is 0.9, the model considers only the most likely tokens that together cover 90% of the probability mass. This makes Top P dynamic because the number of candidate tokens can change at each generation step. In a QA bot, use lower randomness when the answer must be factual and grounded. A common setup is low temperature, top_p around 0.8 to 0.95, and a moderate top_k such as 40 or 50 if the serving API supports it. For creative writing or brainstorming, increase temperature and allow a higher top_p so the model can explore more varied answers. Example generation settings: { "temperature": 0.2, "top_p": 0.9, "top_k": 50, "max_tokens": 500 } Use lower Top P or Top K for more deterministic and safer answers. Use higher Top P or Top K when you want more diverse wording or creative output. Do not increase all randomness settings together for factual QA, because it can increase hallucination risk. learn by QA What is create_delta_sync_index()? create_delta_sync_index() is a Databricks Vector Search function used to create a vector index that automatically stays synchronized with a Delta table. Instead of manually generating and updating embeddings, Databricks continuously monitors the source Delta table and updates the vector index whenever new records are added, updated, or deleted. This is commonly used in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) applications where documents are stored in Delta tables and must be searchable through semantic similarity search. The function creates a managed pipeline that keeps the vector index and source data aligned. Source Documents ↓ Delta Table ↓ create_delta_sync_index() ↓ Vector Search Index ↓ Similarity Search / RAG Retrieval For example, suppose a company stores support articles in a Delta table. After creating a Delta Sync Index, whenever a new article is added, Databricks automatically generates embeddings and updates the vector index. A chatbot can then retrieve the most relevant articles using semantic search without requiring manual index refreshes. Purpose: create a vector search index that automatically syncs with a Delta table. Used in: RAG applications, semantic search, document retrieval, and AI assistants. Benefit: eliminates manual embedding generation and index update workflows. Supports: continuous synchronization when source data changes. Common source: Delta tables containing documents, chunks, embeddings, metadata, or knowledge base content. A simplified example: client.create_delta_sync_index( endpoint_name="vs_endpoint", index_name="catalog.schema.docs_index", source_table_name="catalog.schema.documents", pipeline_type="TRIGGERED" ) In a typical Databricks Gen AI workflow, documents are parsed, chunked, stored in a Delta table, and then create_delta_sync_index() is used to build a searchable vector index that powers retrieval for chatbots and AI agents. What is Sequence Truncation with Token Replacement? Sequence Truncation with Token Replacement is a way to handle input text that is longer than the model's maximum context window. Instead of sending the full sequence, the system shortens it and replaces the removed part with a special marker token or summary token. Normal truncation simply cuts off extra tokens. Token replacement is more informative because it leaves a signal that some content was removed. For example, a long document may be converted into an input like: Introduction text ... [TRUNCATED_SECTION] ... final question This helps the model understand that the sequence is incomplete. In some systems, the replacement token can be a simple placeholder such as [TRUNCATED]. In more advanced systems, it can be a compressed summary, metadata marker, or special token learned during model training. In Gen AI applications, this technique is useful when prompts include long conversation history, retrieved documents, logs, or user-provided files. It helps keep the input within the token budget while preserving the fact that omitted information existed. Sequence truncation: removes tokens that exceed the model's context window or token budget. Token replacement: inserts a marker or compressed representation where content was removed. Main benefit: prevents context window overflow while reducing confusion caused by silent cuts. Main risk: if important details are removed, the model may still miss key facts even though a marker is present. What are Databricks Workflows and their use cases? Databricks Workflows are used to schedule, run, and orchestrate production data and AI jobs in Databricks. A workflow can contain one or more tasks, and each task can run a notebook, Python script, SQL query, JAR, dbt command, Delta Live Tables pipeline, or model training and inference step. A workflow is useful when a process has to run repeatedly, reliably, and in the correct order. Instead of manually running notebooks, you define the steps, dependencies, schedule, compute, parameters, retries, alerts, and failure handling. Example daily sales workflow: 1. Ingest raw sales files from cloud storage 2. Clean and validate the data 3. Merge records into a Delta table 4. Refresh an aggregate reporting table 5. Notify the team if any step fails Workflows are commonly used for ETL pipelines, ELT pipelines, batch data processing, streaming pipeline coordination, ML model training, feature engineering, model scoring, reporting refreshes, and operational automation. ETL example: load customer CSV files, transform them with Spark, and write clean records into silver and gold Delta tables. ML example: prepare training data, train a model, evaluate metrics, register the model in MLflow, and run batch predictions. Reporting example: refresh sales summary tables every morning before dashboards are opened by business users. RAG example: parse new documents, chunk the text, create embeddings, update a vector index, and validate search quality. Main benefit: Databricks manages scheduling, dependencies, retries, monitoring, alerts, and job history in one place. What is NER (Named Entity Recognition)? NER, or Named Entity Recognition, is an NLP technique that finds important named items in text and classifies them into predefined categories. These categories often include person names, organizations, locations, dates, products, money amounts, percentages, and other domain-specific entities. For example, in the sentence Databricks acquired MosaicML in 2023., an NER model may identify Databricks and MosaicML as organizations and 2023 as a date. Input: Satya Nadella works at Microsoft in Redmond. Output: Satya Nadella = PERSON Microsoft = ORGANIZATION Redmond = LOCATION NER is useful when unstructured text needs to be converted into structured data. In data and AI systems, it can help extract entities from customer messages, support tickets, contracts, clinical notes, news articles, resumes, logs, or documents used in search and RAG pipelines. Purpose: detect and label meaningful named items inside text. Common labels: person, organization, location, date, product, money, and percentage. Main benefit: turns free-form text into structured fields that can be searched, filtered, analyzed, or joined with other data. Main risk: entity boundaries and labels can be wrong when names are ambiguous or domain context is missing. What is Unity Catalog's role-based masking? Role-based masking in Unity Catalog is a data governance feature that allows organizations to hide or partially mask sensitive data based on the user's assigned role, group membership, or permissions. Instead of giving every user access to the original values, Unity Catalog can dynamically return masked values while still allowing authorized users to view the full data. For example, a customer table may contain email addresses and credit card information. Data analysts may only see masked values, while administrators or compliance teams can view the actual data. Input Data: Customer = John Smith Email = john.smith@example.com Card No. = 4111-1111-1111-1111 Analyst View: Customer = John Smith Email = j***@example.com Card No. = ****-****-****-1111 Admin View: Customer = John Smith Email = john.smith@example.com Card No. = 4111-1111-1111-1111 Role-based masking helps organizations protect personally identifiable information (PII), financial records, healthcare data, and other sensitive fields while enabling users to perform analytics on the same datasets. Masking policies are centrally managed in Unity Catalog and enforced automatically whenever users query the data. Purpose: protect sensitive columns by showing different values to different users based on their roles or permissions. Common use cases: masking emails, phone numbers, credit card numbers, salaries, customer identifiers, and healthcare records. Main benefit: enables secure data sharing while maintaining a single source of truth for governed datasets. Main risk: incorrectly configured masking policies may expose sensitive information or unnecessarily restrict legitimate access. What is Autoscaling in Cloud Computing? Autoscaling is a cloud computing feature that automatically adjusts computational resources (like CPUs, GPUs, or entire servers) based on real-time system demand without manual intervention. It continuously monitors metrics like CPU usage, memory, or request count, and dynamically adds or removes resources to match workload requirements. For example, during a flash sale on an e-commerce site, traffic might spike 10x. Autoscaling automatically launches additional servers to handle the load, then terminates them when traffic returns to normal. Normal traffic: 2 servers running Flash sale starts (10x traffic): System adds 8 more servers Sale ends: System removes the 8 extra servers → back to 2 servers Autoscaling is essential for running applications efficiently in the cloud. For LLM inference workloads (like RAG applications), it ensures sufficient GPU/compute capacity during high demand while avoiding over-provisioning during low-traffic periods. Purpose: Automatically allocate/deallocate resources based on demand to maintain performance and optimize costs. Two types: Horizontal scaling (adds/removes entire instances) and vertical scaling (increases/decreases CPU/RAM of existing instances). Main benefit: Prevents over-provisioning (paying for unused capacity) while ensuring uptime during traffic spikes. Main risk: Scaling lag (delay between detecting load and adding resources) can cause temporary slowdowns if thresholds are too aggressive. How Does Autoscaling Affect LLM Performance? When autoscaling increases resources for LLM inference, it adds more GPUs or servers to handle the workload. This improves response times and throughput during high demand, but it does NOT change the quality of answers or reduce the per-query cost of using the LLM. For example, with 2 GPUs processing 100 requests/sec, latency might be 500ms with 10 requests queued. After scaling to 8 GPUs, latency drops to 150ms with 0 requests queued—but each query still costs the same per token. Before scaling: 2 GPUs, 100 req/sec → 500ms latency, 10 queued After scaling: 8 GPUs, 100 req/sec → 150ms latency, 0 queued Per-query cost: Still the same (same tokens, same price per token) This distinction is critical for RAG applications. Autoscaling optimizes infrastructure utilization (not wasting idle capacity), but it doesn't reduce the actual LLM inference cost, which is determined by token count and number of queries. What improves: Latency (faster responses), throughput (more concurrent queries), queue time (fewer waiting requests), and availability during traffic spikes. What stays the same: Per-query cost, model quality, response accuracy, and token consumption—autoscaling doesn't make the LLM cheaper or smarter. Main benefit for LLMs: Maintains performance under load without manual intervention, preventing dropped requests during peak usage. Why it doesn't solve cost problems: LLM costs are driven by tokens × queries, not infrastructure. To reduce costs, you need prompt optimization (fewer tokens) or caching (fewer queries), not more GPUs. Best Way to Control RAG Costs in Databricks (Without Losing Quality) The most effective way to control RAG application costs in Databricks without compromising response quality is to employ prompt optimization techniques and cache common query results. Prompt optimization reduces token usage per query, while caching eliminates redundant LLM calls for repeated questions. For example, if users frequently ask "What is the return policy?", caching the response means the LLM only runs once, and subsequent identical queries return the cached answer instantly at near-zero cost. Without caching: User asks "Return policy?" → LLM runs → 500 tokens → $0.01 User asks "Return policy?" → LLM runs → 500 tokens → $0.01 User asks "Return policy?" → LLM runs → 500 tokens → $0.01 Total: 1500 tokens, $0.03 With caching: User asks "Return policy?" → LLM runs → 500 tokens → $0.01 User asks "Return policy?" → Cache hit → 0 tokens → $0 User asks "Return policy?" → Cache hit → 0 tokens → $0 Total: 500 tokens, $0.01 (67% savings) Unlike autoscaling (which optimizes infrastructure but not per-query cost) or reducing max tokens (which risks incomplete answers), prompt optimization + caching directly reduces LLM usage while maintaining full response quality. Prompt optimization: Use concise prompts, reduce top-k documents, summarize retrieved chunks, and encourage brief responses to cut token usage. Caching: Store responses for common/similar queries in Databricks Cache or MLflow to avoid redundant LLM inference. Main benefit: Directly reduces LLM inference costs by lowering token consumption and query count, without sacrificing answer quality. Why other options fail: Autoscaling doesn't reduce per-query cost; checkpointing is for training (not inference); reducing max tokens truncates responses and harms quality. What is Delta Lake? Delta Lake is an open storage layer that adds reliability and database-like behavior to data lakes. It is commonly used in Databricks to build lakehouse tables on cloud storage such as AWS S3, Azure Data Lake Storage, or Google Cloud Storage. A normal data lake may store files such as Parquet, CSV, or JSON, but file-based storage alone can be hard to manage when many jobs are reading and writing at the same time. Delta Lake solves this by adding a transaction log, called the Delta log, that tracks table versions and data changes. Data lake files + Delta transaction log = Delta Lake table storage Because of this log, Delta Lake supports ACID transactions, schema enforcement, schema evolution, updates, deletes, merges, scalable metadata, and time travel. This makes it useful for reliable batch pipelines, streaming pipelines, analytics, machine learning, and BI workloads. Purpose: make data lakes more reliable, consistent, and easier to query. Core idea: store data files plus a transaction log that records every committed change. Main benefit: multiple readers and writers can work with the same data safely. Time travel: query older versions of data for debugging, audits, or rollback. In Databricks: Delta Lake is the default foundation for many managed and external lakehouse tables. What is a Delta table? A Delta table is a table stored in Delta Lake format. It keeps the actual data files, usually Parquet files, together with a transaction log that records every change made to the table. In Databricks, Delta tables are used for reliable lakehouse data. The transaction log makes the table behave more like a database table than a plain folder of files. It supports ACID transactions, schema enforcement, updates, deletes, merges, and time travel. CREATE TABLE sales_delta ( order_id BIGINT, customer_id BIGINT, amount DOUBLE ) USING DELTA; A simple way to remember it: Delta Lake is the storage technology, and a Delta table is a specific table created using that technology. You can query it with SQL, process it with Spark, govern it with Unity Catalog, and use it in ETL, streaming, ML, and BI workloads. Data files: store the table's rows, commonly in Parquet format. Transaction log: tracks versions, commits, schema changes, and data modifications. ACID support: helps reads and writes stay consistent even when multiple jobs run together. Time travel: lets you query or restore older versions of the table. Common use: bronze, silver, and gold tables in Databricks data pipelines. What is MLflow's model signature feature? MLflow's model signature feature records the expected input and output schema of a logged model. It describes what kind of data the model accepts and what kind of prediction or response it returns. A signature can include column names, data types, tensor shapes, or parameter schemas, depending on the model. For example, a classifier might have inputs such as age, income, and country, and an output such as prediction. A generative AI application might define an input field such as question and an output field such as answer. Input schema: question: string Output schema: answer: string The signature is useful because it makes model behavior easier to validate, serve, document, and govern. When the model is deployed, MLflow and Databricks can use the signature to check whether inference requests match the expected format before sending them to the model. Purpose: define the model's input and output contract. Benefit: catches schema mismatches during testing, deployment, and serving. Usage: stored with the MLflow model artifact when the model is logged. Common source: inferred from example inputs and outputs, or manually specified by the developer. What is the architecture of an AI summarization model? The architecture of an AI summarization model is the set of components that convert a long document, conversation, or set of retrieved passages into a shorter version that preserves the most important meaning. Modern summarization systems are usually built around a transformer-based language model. The input text is first cleaned, split into tokens, and passed through attention layers. These layers help the model understand which words, sentences, and sections are most relevant to the final summary. A common abstractive summarization flow looks like this: Source text -> Preprocessing -> Tokenizer -> Transformer encoder/decoder or LLM -> Summary decoder -> Post-processing -> Final summary In an encoder-decoder model, the encoder reads the source text and creates contextual representations. The decoder then generates the summary one token at a time. In a decoder-only LLM, the prompt contains the source text plus summarization instructions, and the model predicts the summary directly as the completion. Production summarization systems often add extra layers around the model. For long documents, a chunking step breaks the input into smaller sections. Each section may be summarized first, and then a second model call combines those partial summaries into a final answer. This is often called map-reduce summarization. Input layer: receives documents, transcripts, chats, emails, or retrieved passages. Preprocessing layer: cleans text, removes noise, splits long inputs, and keeps useful metadata. Tokenization layer: converts text into token IDs that the model can process. Model layer: uses transformer attention to identify important context and generate or select summary content. Decoding layer: controls the generated text using settings such as max tokens, temperature, Top P, and repetition penalties. Post-processing layer: checks formatting, removes duplicates, enforces length, and may validate facts against the source. There are three common summarization architecture patterns: Extractive summarization: selects important sentences from the original text without rewriting them. Abstractive summarization: generates a new, shorter version in natural language, often using different wording from the source. RAG-assisted summarization: retrieves the most relevant chunks first, then summarizes only the grounded context. The main design goal is to balance compression, factual accuracy, coverage, and readability. A good architecture should reduce the amount of text while keeping the key facts, decisions, dates, metrics, and relationships intact. What are BLEU and ROUGE, and when should they be used? BLEU and ROUGE are automatic evaluation metrics used to compare generated text with one or more reference answers written by humans. They are useful when you need a quick, repeatable way to compare model outputs across a test dataset. BLEU stands for Bilingual Evaluation Understudy. It is mostly used for machine translation. BLEU measures how many word sequences, called n-grams, in the generated text also appear in the reference text. It rewards precision, meaning the model gets a better score when the words it produces match the reference. ROUGE stands for Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation. It is mostly used for summarization. ROUGE measures how much of the reference summary is covered by the generated summary. It rewards recall, meaning the model gets a better score when it includes the important words, phrases, or sequences from the reference. Generated text + Reference text -> BLEU / ROUGE metric -> Similarity score Metric Main idea Best used for BLEU Precision-oriented n-gram overlap: how much of the generated text matches the reference wording. Machine translation, controlled text generation, and regression tests where exact phrasing matters. ROUGE Recall-oriented overlap: how much of the reference answer or summary is covered by the generated text. Summarization, content coverage checks, and comparing whether key points were preserved. Use BLEU when evaluating translation or other tasks where exact wording and phrase matching are important. Use ROUGE when evaluating summarization, especially when checking whether key points from a reference summary were included. Use ROUGE-1 to compare individual word overlap between generated and reference summaries. Use ROUGE-2 to compare two-word phrase overlap, which is stricter than ROUGE-1. Use ROUGE-L to compare the longest matching word sequence, which helps measure sentence structure similarity. Avoid using BLEU or ROUGE as the primary metric for open-ended chat, reasoning, code generation, creative writing, or factual QA where multiple very different answers can be correct. These metrics are best for offline model evaluation, benchmark comparisons, regression testing, and choosing between prompt or model versions. For example, if you change a summarization prompt, ROUGE can help check whether the new prompt still preserves the same important content as the reference summaries. BLEU and ROUGE should not be the only way to judge Gen AI quality. They rely heavily on word overlap, so they can penalize a correct answer that uses different wording and reward an answer that copies words but misses deeper meaning. For production systems, combine them with human review, factuality checks, task success metrics, and safety evaluations. This happens because the system relies on its training data, which may be limited or skewed.
