title: JavaScript
content: JavaScript Specific Interview Questions 1. Arrow Function vs Normal Function Definition: Arrow functions do not have their own this, arguments, super, or new.target. They inherit this from lexical scope. Common Interview Questions: Why can't arrow functions be used as constructors? Difference between arrow and normal functions? 2. Closure Definition: A closure allows a function to retain access to outer scope variables even after execution. 3. Hoisting Function declarations → Fully hoisted var → Hoisted as undefined let & const → Temporal Dead Zone console.log(a); // undefined var a = 5; console.log(b); // ReferenceError let b = 10; 4. Promises, Async & Await Promise states: Pending, Fulfilled, Rejected async/await improves readability Difference between Promise.all and Promise.allSettled 5. Pure vs Impure Functions Pure Function: It depends only on its input parameters and not on any external variable , a function accessing outer variable is not a pure JS function. Always returns the same output for the same input. Has no side effects (does not modify external state). function add(a, b) { return a + b; } Impure Function: May produce different output for same input. Has side effects (modifies external variables, DOM, API calls, etc.). let total = 0; function addToTotal(value) { total += value; return total; } Interview Focus: Why are pure functions preferred in functional programming? How do pure functions improve testability and predictability? 6. Debouncing Definition: Debouncing ensures a function runs only after a specified period of inactivity since the last call. Use cases: Search-as-you-type to reduce API calls. Window resize or scroll handlers. Form input validation. Debounce vs Throttle: Debounce waits until the user stops triggering events. Throttle executes at most once per interval during a burst of events. // Simple debounce implementation function debounce(fn, delay) { let timerId; return function (...args) { const context = this; clearTimeout(timerId); timerId = setTimeout(() => fn.apply(context, args), delay); }; } // Example: Debounce input handler const input = document.querySelector('#search'); const onInput = debounce((e) => { console.log('Searching for:', e.target.value); // fetch(`/api/search?q=${encodeURIComponent(e.target.value)}`) }, 300); input.addEventListener('input', onInput);

title: LeetCode & DSA
content: LeetCode & DSA Interview Preparation Notes LeetCode & DSA Topics Pattern recognition matters more than memorizing isolated solutions. Most interview problems reduce to a small set of repeatable moves: tracking state, shrinking a window, splitting a search space, traversing a graph, or building an answer from smaller subproblems. Quick interview workflow: Identify input type: array, string, linked list, tree, graph, matrix, interval, or DP state. Ask what repeats: contiguous range, sorted order, revisits, choices, or optimal substructure. Choose the smallest fitting pattern before coding. Write the template first, then plug in problem-specific conditions. 1. Hash Map / Hash Set Pattern Use when: You need O(1) average lookup, counting, frequency tracking, or seen-before checks. Typical signals: duplicates, complements, counts, grouping, membership. Time complexity: usually O(n). Generic Templates JavaScript function solve(nums) { const map = new Map(); for (let i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) { const value = nums[i]; if (map.has(/* target lookup */)) { return /* answer */; } map.set(value, i); // or increment frequency } return /* fallback */; } C++ int solve(vector<int>& nums) { unordered_map<int, int> mp; for (int i = 0; i < (int)nums.size(); i++) { int value = nums[i]; if (mp.count(/* target lookup */)) { return /* answer */; } mp[value] = i; } return -1; } Examples: 1. Two Sum 217. Contains Duplicate 49. Group Anagrams 2. Two Pointers Pattern Use when: You process a sequence from both ends or maintain two moving positions. Typical signals: sorted array, palindrome, pair sum, partitioning, in-place overwrite. Common forms: opposite-direction pointers, slow-fast pointers, read-write pointers. Generic Templates JavaScript function solve(arr) { let left = 0; let right = arr.length - 1; while (left < right) { const sum = arr[left] + arr[right]; if (sum === /* target */) return [left, right]; if (sum < /* target */) left++; else right--; } return [-1, -1]; } C++ vector<int> solve(vector<int>& arr, int target) { int left = 0, right = (int)arr.size() - 1; while (left < right) { int sum = arr[left] + arr[right]; if (sum == target) return {left, right}; if (sum < target) left++; else right--; } return {-1, -1}; } Examples: 167. Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted 125. Valid Palindrome 11. Container With Most Water 3. Sliding Window Pattern Use when: The answer depends on a contiguous subarray or substring. Typical signals: longest, shortest, at most k, exact k, distinct characters, running sum in a range. Keep expanding the right side and shrink from the left when the window becomes invalid. Generic Templates JavaScript function solve(s) { let left = 0; let best = 0; const freq = new Map(); for (let right = 0; right < s.length; right++) { freq.set(s[right], (freq.get(s[right]) || 0) + 1); while (/* window invalid */) { freq.set(s[left], freq.get(s[left]) - 1); if (freq.get(s[left]) === 0) freq.delete(s[left]); left++; } best = Math.max(best, right - left + 1); } return best; } C++ int solve(string s) { int left = 0, best = 0; unordered_map<char, int> freq; for (int right = 0; right < (int)s.size(); right++) { freq[s[right]]++; while (/* window invalid */) { freq[s[left]]--; if (freq[s[left]] == 0) freq.erase(s[left]); left++; } best = max(best, right - left + 1); } return best; } Examples: 3. Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters 567. Permutation in String 209. Minimum Size Subarray Sum 4. Binary Search Pattern Use when: The search space is sorted or the answer is monotonic: false false false true true true. Typical signals: sorted array, first/last position, minimum valid answer, capacity/speed/days. Variants: exact search, lower bound, upper bound, binary search on answer. Generic Templates JavaScript function binarySearch(nums, target) { let left = 0; let right = nums.length - 1; while (left <= right) { const mid = left + Math.floor((right - left) / 2); if (nums[mid] === target) return mid; if (nums[mid] < target) left = mid + 1; else right = mid - 1; } return -1; } C++ int binarySearch(vector<int>& nums, int target) { int left = 0, right = (int)nums.size() - 1; while (left <= right) { int mid = left + (right - left) / 2; if (nums[mid] == target) return mid; if (nums[mid] < target) left = mid + 1; else right = mid - 1; } return -1; } Examples: 704. Binary Search 33. Search in Rotated Sorted Array 875. Koko Eating Bananas 5. Prefix Sum Pattern Use when: Many queries or decisions depend on sums/counts of subarrays. Typical signals: range sum, subarray count, sum equals k, cumulative balance. Combine with a hash map when you need to count previous prefix values quickly. Generic Templates JavaScript function solve(nums, k) { let prefix = 0; let answer = 0; const count = new Map([[0, 1]]); for (const num of nums) { prefix += num; answer += count.get(prefix - k) || 0; count.set(prefix, (count.get(prefix) || 0) + 1); } return answer; } C++ int solve(vector<int>& nums, int k) { int prefix = 0, answer = 0; unordered_map<int, int> count; count[0] = 1; for (int num : nums) { prefix += num; answer += count[prefix - k]; count[prefix]++; } return answer; } Examples: 303. Range Sum Query - Immutable 560. Subarray Sum Equals K 523. Continuous Subarray Sum 6. Stack / Monotonic Stack Pattern Use when: You need nearest greater/smaller values, undo nesting, or process elements in order with delayed resolution. Typical signals: next greater element, valid parentheses, histogram, daily temperatures. Monotonic stack stores indices or values in increasing or decreasing order. Generic Templates JavaScript function solve(nums) { const stack = []; const answer = Array(nums.length).fill(-1); for (let i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) { while (stack.length && nums[stack[stack.length - 1]] < nums[i]) { const idx = stack.pop(); answer[idx] = nums[i]; } stack.push(i); } return answer; } C++ vector<int> solve(vector<int>& nums) { stack<int> st; vector<int> answer(nums.size(), -1); for (int i = 0; i < (int)nums.size(); i++) { while (!st.empty() && nums[st.top()] < nums[i]) { answer[st.top()] = nums[i]; st.pop(); } st.push(i); } return answer; } Examples: 20. Valid Parentheses 739. Daily Temperatures 84. Largest Rectangle in Histogram 7. Fast and Slow Pointer Pattern Use when: Linked lists or arrays require cycle detection, midpoint finding, or relative pointer movement. Typical signals: detect cycle, middle node, happy number, reorder list, palindrome list. Move slow by 1 step and fast by 2 steps. Generic Templates JavaScript function hasCycle(head) { let slow = head; let fast = head; while (fast && fast.next) { slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next.next; if (slow === fast) return true; } return false; } C++ bool hasCycle(ListNode* head) { ListNode* slow = head; ListNode* fast = head; while (fast && fast->next) { slow = slow->next; fast = fast->next->next; if (slow == fast) return true; } return false; } Examples: 141. Linked List Cycle 876. Middle of the Linked List 202. Happy Number 8. DFS / BFS for Trees and Graphs Use when: You need traversal, component counting, shortest path in unweighted graphs, or level-order processing. DFS: explore completely before backtracking. BFS: visit level by level using a queue. Generic Templates JavaScript DFS function dfs(node, visited, graph) { if (visited.has(node)) return; visited.add(node); for (const nei of graph[node]) { dfs(nei, visited, graph); } } C++ BFS void bfs(int start, vector<vector<int>>& graph, vector<bool>& visited) { queue<int> q; q.push(start); visited[start] = true; while (!q.empty()) { int node = q.front(); q.pop(); for (int nei : graph[node]) { if (!visited[nei]) { visited[nei] = true; q.push(nei); } } } } Examples: 102. Binary Tree Level Order Traversal 200. Number of Islands 133. Clone Graph 9. Heap / Priority Queue Pattern Use when: You repeatedly need the smallest or largest element while the data keeps changing. Typical signals: top k, kth largest, merge sorted lists, scheduling, streaming median. Min-heap for smallest-first, max-heap for largest-first. Generic Templates JavaScript In interviews, JavaScript often needs a custom heap class. The core loop usually looks like this: function process(values, heap) { for (const value of values) { heap.push(value); if (heap.size() > /* k */) heap.pop(); } return heap.peek(); } C++ int solve(vector<int>& nums, int k) { priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>> pq; for (int num : nums) { pq.push(num); if ((int)pq.size() > k) pq.pop(); } return pq.top(); } Examples: 215. Kth Largest Element in an Array 347. Top K Frequent Elements 23. Merge k Sorted Lists 10. Backtracking Pattern Use when: You must try all valid choices and undo them while exploring combinations or permutations. Typical signals: subsets, combinations, permutations, n-queens, word search. Core idea: choose, recurse, un-choose. Generic Templates JavaScript function backtrack(start, path, result, nums) { result.push([...path]); for (let i = start; i < nums.length; i++) { path.push(nums[i]); backtrack(i + 1, path, result, nums); path.pop(); } } C++ void backtrack(int start, vector<int>& path, vector<vector<int>>& result, vector<int>& nums) { result.push_back(path); for (int i = start; i < (int)nums.size(); i++) { path.push_back(nums[i]); backtrack(i + 1, path, result, nums); path.pop_back(); } } Examples: 78. Subsets 39. Combination Sum 79. Word Search 11. Dynamic Programming Pattern Use when: A problem has overlapping subproblems and an optimal answer can be built from smaller states. Typical signals: count ways, minimum cost, maximum profit, decision at each index. First define the state, then transition, then base case. Generic Templates JavaScript function solve(nums) { const n = nums.length; const dp = Array(n + 1).fill(0); dp[0] = 0; for (let i = 1; i <= n; i++) { dp[i] = Math.max( dp[i - 1], (i >= 2 ? dp[i - 2] : 0) + nums[i - 1] ); } return dp[n]; } C++ int solve(vector<int>& nums) { int n = nums.size(); vector<int> dp(n + 1, 0); for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) { dp[i] = max( dp[i - 1], (i >= 2 ? dp[i - 2] : 0) + nums[i - 1] ); } return dp[n]; } Examples: 70. Climbing Stairs 198. House Robber 322. Coin Change 12. Interval Merging Pattern Use when: Ranges overlap, need consolidation, or scheduling depends on sorted start/end times. Typical signals: merge intervals, meeting rooms, insert interval, overlap detection. Standard move: sort first, then merge greedily. Generic Templates JavaScript function merge(intervals) { intervals.sort((a, b) => a[0] - b[0]); const result = []; for (const interval of intervals) { if (!result.length || result[result.length - 1][1] < interval[0]) { result.push(interval); } else { result[result.length - 1][1] = Math.max(result[result.length - 1][1], interval[1]); } } return result; } C++ vector<vector<int>> merge(vector<vector<int>>& intervals) { sort(intervals.begin(), intervals.end()); vector<vector<int>> result; for (auto& interval : intervals) { if (result.empty() || result.back()[1] < interval[0]) { result.push_back(interval); } else { result.back()[1] = max(result.back()[1], interval[1]); } } return result; } Examples: 56. Merge Intervals 57. Insert Interval 435. Non-overlapping Intervals

title: Lovable.dev
content: Lovable.dev Lovable is a full-stack AI development platform for building, iterating, and deploying web applications from natural language prompts. 1. What is Lovable.dev? Lovable helps create web apps and websites by describing the desired product in plain language. It can generate frontend, backend, database, authentication, and integrations depending on the project needs. The output is real editable code, so developers can inspect, change, and connect it to normal engineering workflows. It is useful for prototypes, internal tools, MVPs, landing pages, and fast product experiments. 2. Basic Workflow 2.1 Start with a clear prompt Describe the app goal, target users, main pages, data model, and expected actions. Mention the visual style only after the core workflow is clear. Attach screenshots or references when you want Lovable to match an existing interface. 2.2 Iterate in small steps Ask for one feature or fix at a time instead of rewriting the whole app repeatedly. Review the preview after each change and name exactly what should be different. Keep accepted behavior stable by saying what should not change. 2.3 Connect, test, and deploy Use GitHub integration when you want source control, manual review, or team collaboration. Test authentication, database rules, payments, forms, and edge cases before treating the app as production-ready. Keep secrets, API keys, and permissions out of generated client-side code. 3. Prompting Tips Good first prompt: "Build a task management app for a small team with login, projects, task status, due dates, and a dashboard." Good edit prompt: "On the task detail page, add comments with author name and timestamp. Do not change the dashboard layout." Good debug prompt: "The save button shows success, but refreshed data is missing. Inspect the database write path and fix the root cause." Good design prompt: "Make the table denser for daily use, keep the current colors, and add clear empty and loading states." 4. Strengths and Watchouts 4.1 Strengths Fast path from idea to working app preview. Helpful for non-boilerplate UI generation and quick iteration. Supports a conversational workflow for adding features and fixing issues. GitHub integration can bring generated code into a normal repository workflow. 4.2 Watchouts Generated code still needs developer review for security, performance, accessibility, and maintainability. AI may make assumptions about business rules unless the prompt is specific. Production apps need real testing around auth, data access, payments, and failure states. Keep a habit of committing useful milestones so you can recover from unwanted changes. 5. Learn Lovable by QA Can we run Lovable locally? Lovable itself runs as a cloud platform, but the generated project code can be worked on locally after connecting the project to GitHub. Connect the Lovable project to GitHub. Clone the GitHub repository to your machine. Install dependencies and run the app locally based on the generated project's README or package scripts. Push commits back to GitHub so changes can sync with Lovable. How to connect Lovable with Supabase backend and database? Lovable can connect to Supabase to use PostgreSQL database, authentication, storage, real-time updates, and backend edge functions. You can also use Lovable Cloud, which provides a built-in backend based on Supabase without separate setup. Create or sign in to your Supabase account. Create a Supabase project, or let Lovable guide you while connecting. In Lovable, open the project editor and go to Integrations or project settings. Select Supabase, click Connect Supabase, sign in, and authorize Lovable. Choose the Supabase organization and project to link with your Lovable app. Wait until Lovable confirms that Supabase is connected. To connect app screens with database tables, describe the data feature in Lovable chat. For example: "Create a feedback form and save name, email, rating, and message in Supabase." Lovable can suggest the table structure and SQL. Run the SQL in Supabase SQL Editor, then confirm in Lovable so it can wire the UI to the database. Use Supabase Table Editor to view and edit rows. Use SQL Editor for table creation, schema updates, and custom queries. Use Authentication for users and login flows. Use Storage for files such as images and documents. Use Edge Functions when the app needs secure backend logic or API secrets. How can I see backend tables? If your Lovable project is connected to Supabase, you can see backend tables from the Supabase dashboard. Open your Supabase dashboard. Select the project connected with Lovable. Go to Table Editor from the left menu. Select a table to view columns, rows, and relationships. Use SQL Editor if you want to inspect tables with SQL queries. In Lovable, you can also ask: "Show me the database schema" or "Which Supabase tables does this app use?" to understand how the app is connected to the backend. How to create a copy of a Lovable project? In Lovable, creating a copy of a project is called remixing. A remix creates a separate copy in the currently selected workspace, while the original project stays unchanged. Open the Lovable project you want to copy. Click the project name at the top left of the editor. Open Project Settings. Select Remix. Choose the workspace where the copied project should be created. You can remix your own projects or projects you have access to. Other users can remix your project only if remixing/access is enabled for them. If the project uses Lovable Cloud backend, backend structure like tables and edge functions can be copied, but existing data and manually added secrets are not copied. Projects connected to Supabase may have remix limitations, especially when remixing projects owned by other users. When we create a copy using Remix, does it copy the database also? Remix copies the project code and app structure, but it does not copy the actual database rows or user data. If the project uses Lovable Cloud backend, the backend structure such as tables and edge functions can be copied, but the data inside those tables is not copied. Automatically generated keys are recreated for the copied project. Manually added secrets or keys are not copied. If the project is connected to Supabase, you may need to connect the remixed project to its own Supabase project and recreate or migrate the database schema/data manually. For production data, export and import the database separately using Supabase tools or SQL backups. 6. Useful Links Lovable official website Lovable documentation Getting started guide GitHub integration guide

title: React.js
content:
React.js Interview Preparation Notes to do -- add add about suspense in react.js https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wIyHSOugGGw&list=PPSV and https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6MYc-ZewjVU&list=PLkMY2Sn6ZX3m_YIbwL8vNos98pkpeIzaD&index=42 redux thunk , redux saga , react routing , lazy loading , memorization . How React.js Works Internally (Core Concepts) 1️⃣ Virtual DOM React creates a lightweight copy of the real DOM called the Virtual DOM. Whenever state or props change, React updates the Virtual DOM first. It compares the updated Virtual DOM with the previous version. 2️⃣ Diffing Algorithm (Reconciliation) React uses a smart diffing algorithm to compare old and new Virtual DOM trees. Instead of re-rendering the entire DOM, React finds only the changed nodes. This process is called Reconciliation. React uses keys to efficiently track list changes. 3️⃣ Rendering Process State/Props change → Virtual DOM updates Diffing happens Only changed elements are updated in the real DOM This makes React fast and efficient. 4️⃣ Fiber Architecture React Fiber is the internal engine of React. It allows incremental rendering. Supports features like Concurrent Mode. Improves UI responsiveness. 5️⃣ Why Babel is Used Browsers do not understand JSX directly. Babel converts JSX into regular JavaScript that browsers can execute. Babel also transpiles modern ES6+ syntax to widely supported JavaScript. Often used together with Webpack via babel-loader. Example (JSX to JS): JSX:

Hello

Converted by Babel: React.createElement("h1", null, "Hello"); 6️⃣ Webpack in React (Bundling) Webpack is a module bundler that builds a dependency graph starting from entry points. It bundles JS, CSS, images, etc., into optimized assets for the browser. Uses loaders to transform files (e.g., babel-loader for JSX/TS, css-loader/style-loader for CSS). Plugins extend capabilities (e.g., HtmlWebpackPlugin, DefinePlugin, MiniCssExtractPlugin). DevServer enables HMR (Hot Module Replacement) for fast local development. // webpack.config.js (basic example) const path = require('path'); module.exports = { mode: 'development', entry: './src/index.jsx', output: { path: path.resolve(__dirname, 'dist'), filename: 'bundle.js', publicPath: '/', }, module: { rules: [ { test: /\.jsx?$/i, exclude: /node_modules/, use: { loader: 'babel-loader', options: { presets: ['@babel/preset-env', '@babel/preset-react'] } } }, { test: /\.css$/i, use: ['style-loader', 'css-loader'] } ] }, resolve: { extensions: ['.js', '.jsx'] }, devServer: { historyApiFallback: true, hot: true } }; React.js Specific Interview Questions 1. Functional vs Class Components Class components use lifecycle methods. Functional components use Hooks. Modern React prefers functional components. 2. Hooks 1️⃣ useState Used to manage local component state. Triggers re-render when state updates. import { useState } from "react"; function Counter() { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return ( ); } 2️⃣ useReducer Used for complex state logic. Better for managing multiple related state values. Similar to Redux reducer pattern. import { useReducer } from "react"; function reducer(state, action) { switch(action.type) { case "increment": return { count: state.count + 1 }; default: return state; } } function Counter() { const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, { count: 0 }); return ( ); } 3️⃣ useContext Used to consume values from Context API. Avoids prop drilling. import { useContext } from "react"; import { ThemeContext } from "./ThemeContext"; function Button() { const theme = useContext(ThemeContext); return ; } 4️⃣ useRef Access DOM elements directly. Stores mutable values without re-rendering. import { useRef } from "react"; function InputFocus() { const inputRef = useRef(); const focusInput = () => { inputRef.current.focus(); }; return ( <> ); } 5️⃣ useEffect Used for side effects (API calls, subscriptions, timers). Runs after render. Dependency array controls execution. import { useEffect, useState } from "react"; function Timer() { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); useEffect(() => { const interval = setInterval(() => { setCount(prev => prev + 1); }, 1000); return () => clearInterval(interval); }, []); return

{count}

; } 6️⃣ useMemo Memoizes expensive computed values. Prevents unnecessary recalculations. import { useMemo } from "react"; function Expensive({ num }) { const squared = useMemo(() => { console.log("Calculating..."); return num * num; }, [num]); return

Result: {squared}

; } 7️⃣ useCallback Memoizes function references. Prevents unnecessary re-renders of child components. import { useCallback } from "react"; function Parent() { const handleClick = useCallback(() => { console.log("Clicked"); }, []); return ; } 🔥 Interview Summary useState → Simple state useReducer → Complex state logic useContext → Global data useRef → DOM access / mutable values useEffect → Side effects useMemo → Memoize values useCallback → Memoize functions 3. Context API Context API allows sharing global data without prop drilling. Create using React.createContext() Wrap with Provider Consume using useContext() 4. Pure vs Impure Components Pure Component Same output for same props & state. No side effects inside render. Predictable and easy to test. function Greeting({ name }) { return

Hello {name}

; } Impure Component Output may change without prop/state change. Uses external variables or random values. let count = 0; function Counter() { count++; return

Count: {count}

; } React.PureComponent vs React.memo Feature React.PureComponent React.memo() Used With Class Components Functional Components Optimization Type Shallow comparison of props & state Shallow comparison of props only Syntax class MyComp extends React.PureComponent export default React.memo(MyComp) Custom Comparison No built-in custom comparison Can pass custom comparison function Modern Usage Less preferred now Preferred in modern React Interview Tip: Prefer React.memo in modern React applications since functional components are the standard now. 5. React Portals What are Portals? Portals allow rendering a component outside its parent DOM hierarchy while keeping it inside the same React component tree. Used for Modals, Tooltips, Dropdowns, Overlays. Helps avoid CSS overflow / z-index issues. Still supports event bubbling through React tree. How Portals Work Instead of rendering inside #root, you render into another DOM node. Step 1: Add a div in index.html
Step 2: Create Portal Component import ReactDOM from "react-dom"; function Modal({ children }) { return ReactDOM.createPortal(
{children}
, document.getElementById("portal-root") ); } Step 3: Use It function App() { return ( <>

Main App

This is rendered using Portal

 ); } Key Interview Points Portals do NOT break React’s event bubbling. They only change DOM placement, not React hierarchy. Useful for UI layers (modals, popups). Interview Tip: Portals solve UI layering problems without breaking React’s component structure. 6. Redux Thunk and Redux Saga Redux Thunk Thunk is a middleware that lets you write action creators returning a function instead of an action. Great for simple async flows: dispatch before/after API calls. Lower learning curve and smaller bundle size. // actions.js export const fetchUsers = () => async (dispatch, getState) => { dispatch({ type: 'USERS_REQUEST' }); try { const res = await fetch('/api/users'); const data = await res.json(); dispatch({ type: 'USERS_SUCCESS', payload: data }); } catch (err) { dispatch({ type: 'USERS_FAILURE', error: err.message }); } }; // store.js import { applyMiddleware, createStore } from 'redux'; import thunk from 'redux-thunk'; import rootReducer from './reducer'; const store = createStore(rootReducer, applyMiddleware(thunk)); Redux Saga Saga is a middleware for managing complex async side effects using generator functions. Provides powerful effects like takeEvery, takeLatest, call, put, and cancellation. Great for orchestrating flows, retries, debouncing, and complex business logic. // sagas.js import { call, put, takeLatest } from 'redux-saga/effects'; function* fetchUsersWorker() { try { const res = yield call(fetch, '/api/users'); const data = yield call([res, 'json']); yield put({ type: 'USERS_SUCCESS', payload: data }); } catch (err) { yield put({ type: 'USERS_FAILURE', error: err.message }); } } export function* usersSaga() { yield takeLatest('USERS_REQUEST', fetchUsersWorker); } // store.js import createSagaMiddleware from 'redux-saga'; import { applyMiddleware, createStore } from 'redux'; import rootReducer from './reducer'; import { usersSaga } from './sagas'; const sagaMiddleware = createSagaMiddleware(); const store = createStore(rootReducer, applyMiddleware(sagaMiddleware)); sagaMiddleware.run(usersSaga); Thunk is a Redux middleware that allows action creators to return a function instead of an action object. Redux Thunk is middleware that allows action creators to return functions instead of objects, enabling asynchronous logic like API calls before dispatching actions. When to Choose Which? Thunk: Simple async needs, few API calls, minimal orchestration. Saga: Complex workflows, background tasks, cancellation, debouncing, sequencing. Interview Tip: Start with Thunk for simplicity; move to Saga when side-effect complexity grows and testability/orchestration become priorities. 7. Additional React.js Specific Interview Questions why do we need react ? react uses diffing algorithm , it is easy to use what is virtual dom ? how virtual dom looks like ? why do react uses 2 virtual dom and 1 real DOM and not 1 virtual dom and 1 real dom ? whenever a state changes in react component the component re-renders . what is useContext ? can use we dispatch actions also ? can we use it with useReducer ? why is vite faster than CRA ? CRA uses webpack and babel ? compiling takes time . vite does not bundle while development , it bundle only at time of deployment , during bundle it uses ES6 modules to serve all the 10 files . Vite is new style , CRA is old style, at time of CRA ES6 was not there . what is source map ? helpful for debugging

title: SDE Level 2
content: SDE Level 2 Syllabus & Detailed Notes 1. Syllabus Overview Data Structures & Algorithms System Design (LLD + HLD) Concurrency & Multithreading Database Systems Operating Systems Networking Basics Software Engineering Practices Behavioral & Leadership 2. Data Structures & Algorithms Topics Arrays, Strings Linked List Stack & Queue Trees (BST, AVL) Graphs (BFS, DFS) Heap / Priority Queue Dynamic Programming Notes Time Complexity: Always optimize brute force → O(n log n) or O(n) Heap Usage: Use for Top K problems, priority-based selection Graph: BFS → shortest path, DFS → traversal DP: Identify overlapping subproblems + memoization Important Problem: Top K Frequent Elements Problem: Given an array of integers, return the k most frequent elements. Key Points Order does not matter Focus on frequency, not value Works with duplicates and negative numbers Approach 1: Sorting (O(n log n)) Count frequency using HashMap Convert map → array Sort by frequency (descending) Return first k elements function topKFrequent(nums, k) { const freqMap = new Map(); for (let num of nums) { freqMap.set(num, (freqMap.get(num) || 0) + 1); } const arr = Array.from(freqMap.entries()); arr.sort((a, b) => b[1] - a[1]); return arr.slice(0, k).map(item => item[0]); } Time: O(n log n) Approach 2: Min Heap (O(n log k)) Use a min heap of size k Remove smallest frequency when size exceeds k Keeps only top k elements Time: O(n log k) Approach 3: Bucket Sort (O(n)) Build frequency map Create buckets (index = frequency) Store elements in buckets Traverse from highest frequency function topKFrequent(nums, k) { const freqMap = new Map(); for (let num of nums) { freqMap.set(num, (freqMap.get(num) || 0) + 1); } const buckets = Array(nums.length + 1).fill().map(() => []); for (let [num, freq] of freqMap.entries()) { buckets[freq].push(num); } const result = []; for (let i = buckets.length - 1; i >= 0 && result.length < k; i--) { result.push(...buckets[i]); } return result.slice(0, k); } Time: O(n) Interview Tip Start with sorting (baseline) Optimize using heap Mention bucket sort for best answer 3. System Design Topics Scalability (Horizontal vs Vertical) Load Balancers Caching (Redis, CDN) Database Scaling (Sharding, Replication) Microservices Notes CAP Theorem: Choose between Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance Caching: Reduces DB load, improves latency Rate Limiter: Token bucket / sliding window Design Tip: Always start with requirements → scale later 4. Concurrency & Multithreading Topics Threads vs Processes Synchronization (Mutex, Semaphore) Deadlocks Race Conditions Notes Race Condition: Multiple threads modifying shared data Deadlock: Circular wait → avoid using ordering Thread Pool: Improves performance 5. Database Systems Topics SQL vs NoSQL Indexing Transactions (ACID) Normalization Notes Index: Improves read performance but slows writes ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability NoSQL: Use for scalability and flexible schema 6. Operating Systems Process Scheduling (FCFS, Round Robin) Memory Management Paging & Segmentation Notes Context Switching: CPU switching between processes Virtual Memory: Uses disk as RAM 7. Networking Basics HTTP / HTTPS TCP vs UDP DNS Notes TCP: Reliable but slower UDP: Faster but unreliable HTTPS: Secure using SSL/TLS 8. Behavioral & Leadership Ownership & Responsibility Handling Production Issues Mentoring Juniors Conflict Resolution Notes Use STAR method (Situation, Task, Action, Result) Focus on real production experience 9. SDE roadmap this video provides roadmap - https://www.instagram.com/reels/DSM7ZZIiR8A/ classes , object encapsulation inheritance and composition polymorphism interfaces and abstract classes SOLID principals https://www.linkedin.com/posts/abhay-singh-a64b89192_how-i-switched-to-sde-2-with-multiple-offers-activity-7393945179382190080-1f2y?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAACv5yREBv9N0r8wAMG8gFLfkAuo8i3ewF6g 10. Resources refactoring guru

title: Techi Tech
content: Techi Tech Supabase What is Supabase? Supabase is an open-source backend platform built around PostgreSQL. It provides database, authentication, storage, real-time subscriptions, and serverless edge functions. It is commonly used as a backend for web apps, mobile apps, prototypes, and AI-generated applications. Key Features Database: Managed PostgreSQL with tables, SQL editor, relationships, and policies. Auth: User sign-up, login, OAuth providers, magic links, and session handling. Storage: File buckets for images, documents, and user-uploaded assets. Realtime: Listen to database changes and update the UI instantly. Edge Functions: Run secure backend logic close to users. Common Use Cases Adding login and user profiles to an application. Saving form submissions, dashboard data, or app records in PostgreSQL. Building admin panels, internal tools, SaaS apps, and MVPs. Connecting generated apps from tools like Lovable to a real backend. Important Watchouts Always configure Row Level Security policies before exposing tables to users. Never place service role keys in frontend code. Use migrations or SQL scripts to track important schema changes. Test authentication, permissions, and file access before production use. Slack Slackbot What is Slackbot? Slackbot is Slack's built-in assistant for reminders, automated responses, and simple workspace help. It can send direct messages, remind users about tasks, and answer custom trigger phrases configured by workspace admins. It is useful for lightweight automation that does not need a full Slack app or workflow. Common Uses Creating personal reminders with commands like /remind. Sharing quick answers for repeated workspace questions. Pointing teammates to common links, policies, forms, or support channels. Sending helpful onboarding notes when someone uses a specific keyword or phrase. Tips Keep Slackbot responses short so they are easy to scan inside busy channels. Use clear trigger phrases to avoid accidental or noisy replies. Review custom responses regularly so links, policies, and team details stay current. Use Slack workflows or Slack apps when the automation needs approvals, integrations, or complex logic. Apache Spark What is Apache Spark? Apache Spark is an open-source distributed computing engine for processing large amounts of data. It can run batch jobs, streaming jobs, SQL queries, machine learning workloads, and graph processing. Spark is designed to split work across many machines, process data in parallel, and handle data that is too large for a single computer. Core Ideas Cluster: A group of machines that work together to process data. Driver: The main program that creates Spark jobs and coordinates the work. Executors: Worker processes that run tasks and store intermediate data. DataFrame: A table-like distributed data structure with rows and columns. Lazy evaluation: Spark waits to run transformations until an action, such as count() or show(), is called. Main Components Spark Core: Handles scheduling, memory management, fault recovery, and distributed task execution. Spark SQL: Lets users query structured data using SQL or DataFrame APIs. Structured Streaming: Processes real-time data streams using the same style as batch DataFrames. MLlib: Provides common machine learning algorithms and utilities. GraphX: Supports graph processing and graph-parallel computation. Common Use Cases Cleaning, transforming, and joining large datasets. Running analytics queries on data lakes and warehouses. Building ETL pipelines for batch or streaming data. Preparing data for dashboards, reports, and machine learning models. Processing logs, clickstream events, sensor data, and financial records at scale.

title: Technological Concepts
content: Technological Concepts Webhooks A webhook is a mechanism that allows one application to automatically send data to another application when a specific event happens. Instead of asking a server again and again whether something changed, the receiving system gives the sending system a URL, and the sending system calls that URL when the event occurs. In simple terms, polling means "keep checking for updates," while a webhook means "notify me immediately when an update happens." This makes webhooks useful for event-driven communication between systems. Why webhooks are used Webhooks are used when one system needs to react quickly to changes in another system. They reduce unnecessary network requests and help keep systems synchronized in near real time. Common examples: GitHub sends a webhook when code is pushed to a repository. A payment gateway sends a webhook when a payment succeeds or fails. An e-commerce platform sends a webhook when an order is created. A messaging platform sends a webhook when a new message arrives. How webhooks work An application exposes an HTTP endpoint such as /webhooks/github. The endpoint URL is registered in another system. An event happens in the source system. The source system sends an HTTP request, usually POST, to the registered URL. The destination system validates the request and processes the payload. The destination system returns an HTTP response code to acknowledge receipt. The payload often contains structured JSON data describing the event, such as event type, timestamp, object identifiers, and related metadata. Main components of a webhook Event source: The system that generates the event. Webhook URL: The endpoint that receives the notification. Event payload: The data sent with the webhook request. Headers: Metadata such as content type, signature, or event name. Receiver logic: The code that validates and handles the incoming request. Webhook example flow Assume GitHub is connected to a deployment service. When a developer pushes code to the main branch, GitHub triggers a webhook and sends repository and commit details to the deployment service. The deployment service verifies the request, checks the branch, and starts the deployment pipeline automatically. Webhook payload example { "event": "push", "repository": "sample-project", "branch": "main", "pushed_by": "developer1", "timestamp": "2026-04-05T10:30:00Z" } Real payloads are usually larger and include IDs, URLs, sender information, and detailed object data. Webhook vs API polling Polling: The client repeatedly asks the server whether new data is available. Webhook: The server pushes data to the client when an event occurs. Comparison: Polling can waste requests if nothing changes. Webhooks are more efficient for event-based updates. Polling is simpler when the receiver cannot expose a public endpoint. Webhooks are better when timely updates are important. Advantages of webhooks Near real-time event delivery. Less unnecessary traffic compared to frequent polling. Good fit for automation workflows. Useful for integrating independent services. Challenges and risks The receiving endpoint must be available and reachable. Requests can be retried, so duplicate processing must be handled safely. Payloads must be validated because the endpoint is exposed over HTTP. Failures in downstream systems can delay or block processing. Order of events is not always guaranteed. Security best practices Use HTTPS so payloads are encrypted in transit. Verify signatures or shared secrets sent in request headers. Validate the payload schema before processing it. Authenticate the event source whenever the provider supports it. Log requests and failures for debugging and auditing. Protect against replay attacks when timestamps or delivery IDs are available. Reliability best practices Return a fast success response after basic validation. Process heavy work asynchronously through queues or background jobs. Store delivery IDs to detect duplicate events. Implement idempotent handlers so repeated deliveries do not break data. Keep retry handling in mind because many providers retry failed deliveries. Typical HTTP response codes 200 OK: Event received and accepted. 202 Accepted: Event received and queued for processing. 400 Bad Request: Invalid payload or missing required data. 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden: Authentication or signature validation failed. 500 Internal Server Error: Receiver failed to process the event. Where webhooks are commonly used CI/CD pipelines Payment status updates CRM and marketing automation Chat and notification systems Inventory and order management Third-party SaaS integrations Summary A webhook is an event notification mechanism in which one system sends an HTTP request to another system when something important happens. It is widely used for automation, integrations, and real-time updates. To use webhooks effectively, focus on security, validation, retry handling, and idempotent processing.

title: Testing
content: Software Testing Testing validates that software behaves as expected, prevents regressions, and improves confidence during delivery. The sections below use nested accordions so you can expand from broad testing concepts into techniques, execution types, and practical best practices. Types of Testing Testing can be grouped by technical scope, business purpose, and execution style. Testing Levels Unit Testing: Verifies small isolated units such as functions, classes, or methods. Integration Testing: Validates interaction between modules, services, databases, or APIs. System Testing: Checks the complete application as an integrated whole. End-to-End (E2E) Testing: Validates complete user journeys across the UI, backend services, and data layer in production-like flows. Acceptance Testing: Confirms the product meets business needs and release criteria. Testing by Purpose Functional Testing: Confirms features behave according to requirements. Regression Testing: Ensures existing features still work after code changes. Smoke Testing: Performs a quick high-level validation on major flows. Sanity Testing: Narrowly checks a specific fix or updated area. Exploratory Testing: Uses human investigation to uncover unexpected issues. Non-Functional Testing Performance Testing: Measures response time, throughput, and system stability. Security Testing: Finds vulnerabilities, access issues, and data exposure risks. Usability Testing: Evaluates how easily users can complete tasks. Compatibility Testing: Validates behavior across devices, browsers, and environments. Testing Approaches Manual vs Automation Manual Testing: Best for exploratory, usability, and rapidly changing features. Automated Testing: Best for repeatable validations, regression coverage, and CI pipelines. End-to-End Automation: Useful for validating critical business flows such as login, checkout, or form submission across integrated systems. Balanced Strategy: Automate stable and repetitive checks, keep human effort for discovery and UX judgment. Test Design Techniques Equivalence Partitioning: Divide inputs into meaningful groups and test one value from each. Boundary Value Analysis: Focus on edges where defects often occur. Decision Table Testing: Validate combinations of rules and outcomes. State Transition Testing: Verify behavior when moving between states. Testing Lifecycle and Best Practices Core Lifecycle Steps Understand requirements and identify risks. Plan the test scope, environments, and strategy. Design test cases and prepare test data. Execute tests and document actual results. Log defects, retest fixes, and run regression checks. Summarize coverage, quality status, and release readiness. Recommended Best Practices Write clear, independent, and repeatable test cases. Prioritize high-risk and business-critical flows first. Keep test data realistic and environment setup stable. Integrate automated tests into CI/CD pipelines. Review flaky tests quickly to preserve trust in automation. Track both defect counts and escaped production issues. Common Tools Unit: JUnit, NUnit, Jest, xUnit UI Automation: Selenium, Playwright, Cypress API Testing: Postman, Rest Assured, Newman Performance: JMeter, k6, Gatling Defect Tracking: Jira, Azure DevOps A/B Testing A/B testing compares two or more versions of a product experience with real users to learn which version performs better against a measurable goal. Core Concepts Control: The existing version used as the baseline. Variant: The changed version being tested against the control. Random Assignment: Users are split into groups to reduce selection bias. Primary Metric: The main success measure, such as conversion rate, sign-ups, revenue, or click-through rate. Guardrail Metrics: Safety checks that ensure the change does not harm latency, errors, retention, support tickets, or user satisfaction. Experiment Process Define a clear hypothesis and business objective. Select the target audience, traffic split, and experiment duration. Choose primary, secondary, and guardrail metrics before launch. Run the test long enough to collect reliable data. Analyze results for practical impact, not only statistical significance. Ship the winning version, iterate, or stop if the result is inconclusive. User Assignment Strategies Users can be divided into experiment groups in several ways, depending on how stable, controlled, and targeted the test needs to be. The goal is to make sure each user consistently sees the same version while the experiment is running, and that the assignment can be measured later in analytics. Common Ways to Divide Users Pure Random Assignment: Each eligible user is randomly placed into control or variant groups at assignment time. This reduces selection bias, but the result must be stored in a cookie, database, or user profile so the user does not switch versions on every visit. Deterministic Hash-Based Bucketing: A stable identifier, such as user ID or device ID, is passed through a hash function and mapped to a bucket. The same identifier always produces the same assignment, so the user consistently sees the same version. Experiment-Specific Deterministic Bucketing: The experiment key is included in the hash input, such as checkout_button_copy_v2:user_123. This lets the same user be in different buckets for different experiments while staying stable inside each experiment. Database-Persisted Assignment: The system stores the assigned variant in a database table or user profile after the first assignment. This gives strong consistency and auditability, especially when assignments must be shared across frontend, backend, email, and mobile experiences. Hybrid Approach (Hashing + Persistence): The system first uses hashing to calculate a stable assignment, then persists that result. This combines predictable initial assignment with a durable record for analytics, debugging, customer support, and future analysis. Percentage-Based Bucketing: Users are mapped into numeric buckets such as 0-99, then assigned by rollout percentage. For example, buckets 0-9 can receive a 10% variant rollout, while the remaining buckets stay in control. Feature Flags: A feature-flag system decides which version a user sees. The flag can be configured by percentage, country, plan, user role, or allowlist, and can be turned off quickly without redeploying code. Cookie or Session-Based Assignment: Anonymous users are grouped using browser cookies or session identifiers. This is useful before login, but users may switch groups if cookies are cleared, browsers change, or devices change. Cohort-Based Assignment: Users are grouped by shared traits such as region, device type, plan, signup date, or customer segment. This is helpful when testing a feature for a specific audience, but the analysis should account for the fact that cohorts may behave differently. Geo-Based Assignment: Different countries, states, cities, or data regions receive different versions. This is useful for market-specific rollouts, but geography can introduce external factors such as language, pricing, holidays, and traffic source differences. Account or Organization-Level Assignment: All users in the same company, workspace, tenant, or household see the same version. This avoids inconsistent experiences inside shared accounts and is common in B2B SaaS products. Invite or Allowlist Assignment: Only selected users, beta customers, employees, or internal testers receive the variant. This is useful for early validation before a wider A/B test, but it should not be treated as unbiased experiment data. Implementation Approach Define the experiment: Give the experiment a stable key such as checkout_button_copy_v2, define the variants such as control and variant, and decide the traffic split. Choose the assignment unit: Decide whether assignment should happen by user ID, account ID, device ID, or anonymous cookie. Use account ID when multiple users share the same workspace or organization. Check eligibility: Apply targeting rules before assignment, such as country, plan, platform, app version, or login status. Users who are not eligible should stay outside the experiment. Assign a stable bucket: Convert the assignment unit and experiment key into a deterministic number, usually with a hash function. Map that number to a variant based on the traffic split. Persist the assignment: Store the chosen variant in a database, feature-flag platform, cookie, local storage, or analytics identity profile so the user keeps seeing the same version. Render the correct version: The application reads the assignment and shows the control or variant experience. Avoid changing the assignment midway through a user journey. Track exposure and outcomes: Log an exposure event when the user actually sees the experiment, then track conversion, revenue, retention, errors, latency, and other success or guardrail metrics. Analyze and decide: Compare results between groups after enough data is collected. Decide whether to ship, roll back, continue testing, or run a follow-up experiment. Simple Bucketing Example A common implementation is deterministic hashing. The same user and experiment key always produce the same bucket, so assignment stays stable across sessions. function hashToBucket(value) { let hash = 0; for (let i = 0; i < value.length; i++) { hash = (hash * 31 + value.charCodeAt(i)) % 100; } return hash; } function getVariant(userId, experimentKey) { const input = `${experimentKey}:${userId}`; const bucket = hashToBucket(input); // Stable number from 0 to 99 if (bucket < 50) { return "control"; } return "variant"; } For a 10% rollout, buckets 0-9 can receive the variant and the rest can stay in control. For a 50/50 test, buckets 0-49 can be control and 50-99 can be variant. Feature Flag Flow Create a flag for the experiment, such as new_checkout_enabled. Add targeting rules, such as only logged-in users on the latest app version. Configure the rollout percentage or variant split in the flag system. Read the flag value in the frontend or backend before rendering the feature. Send the assigned variant with analytics events so results can be grouped correctly. Increase rollout, pause the experiment, or disable the variant based on the results. Important Implementation Notes Use stable assignment: A user should not see control on one visit and variant on the next unless the experiment intentionally changes. Do not assign after conversion: Assignment should happen before the user can complete the measured action, otherwise results can be biased. Track exposure separately: Being eligible is not the same as seeing the experiment. Measure users who were actually exposed to the tested experience. Avoid mixed experiences: Keep assignment consistent across frontend, backend, emails, notifications, and mobile apps when they are part of the same journey. Handle anonymous-to-logged-in users: Decide how to merge a cookie-based assignment with a logged-in user ID so the user does not unexpectedly switch versions. Prevent overlapping conflicts: Avoid running multiple experiments that change the same screen, price, recommendation, or checkout flow at the same time. Keep an audit trail: Store the experiment key, variant, start date, end date, targeting rules, and rollout changes so the result can be explained later. Best Practices and Pitfalls Test one meaningful change at a time when you need clear cause and effect. Avoid stopping early just because the result looks positive after a small sample. Check that user assignment is stable so the same user keeps seeing the same version. Segment results by device, geography, traffic source, or user type when behavior may differ. Watch for novelty effects, seasonality, tracking bugs, and overlapping experiments. Document the hypothesis, result, decision, and follow-up learning for future teams.
